Oxalate pKa2≈3.8 is in SRD‑46, but no vlm rows with CaC2O4° logβ=3.2–3.3 were shown in the provided tool results.
Malonate pKa values are available in SRD‑46, but no CaMal° logβ=2.31 entry (with T/I conditions) was provided in the tool results.
Succinic acid pKa data appear in SRD‑46, but no vlm rows with CaSucc° logβ in the 2.0–2.4 range were shown.
Tartaric acid pKa data appear in SRD‑46, but no CaTar° logβ=2.7–3.0 entries (with T/I) were included in the tool outputs.
Citric acid pKa values are present, but no vlm rows listing CaCit− logβ=3.4–3.6 were provided in the tool results.
Carbonate pKa2≈9.91 is in SRD‑46, but no CaCO3° logβ=3.2 value rows (with T/I) were shown in the tool results.
No SRD‑46 tool output here reports H2PO4−/HPO4^2− pKa2≈7.20 or CaPO4− logβ=4.5–6.0 for the stated conditions; no vlm value rows were shown.
Glycine pKa values are present in SRD‑46, but no vlm rows with Ca(Gly)+ logβ=1.56 at I≈0.1 M were included in the tool results.
IDA pKa values are present, but no Ca–IDA (CaL) logβ=3.44–3.47 entries were shown; no vlm rows with logK/T/I were provided.
NTA pKa data exist, but the tool results did not include vlm rows listing Ca–NTA (CaL) logβ=6.41 at I≈0.1 M.
EDTA pKa subset differs in SRD‑46 and no vlm rows with CaY2− logβ=10.65 at I≈0.1 M were provided in the tool outputs.

paragraphs.text: Is there a correlation between ligand basicity (pKa) and Ca2+ binding? Within a given donor class and similar denticity, a positive trend is visible: more basic, more fully deprotonated O donors at the working pH generally give larger Ca2+ logβ. For example, monocarboxylate acetate (pKa 4.76) binds weakly (logβ1 ≈ 0.6–0.8), while bidentate dicarboxylates and multidentate polycarboxylates escalate to tartrate/citrate (logβ1 ≈ 2.7–3.6) and then to aminopolycarboxylates NTA/EDTA (logβ ≈ 6.4 → 10.7). However, across different families, basicity alone is not predictive: oxalate (lower pKa2 ≈ 3.8) still outperforms malonate/succinate due to tight O,O chelation; phosphates (lower pKa2 than carbonate) bind Ca2+ more strongly because of higher charge density and favorable hard‑hard interactions, and polyphosphates/phosphonates gain further strength via multidentate binding and multiple negative charges.

paragraphs.text: Hypothesis for alkaline‑earth metals: Binding strength increases with effective oxygen‑donor basicity and total negative charge at the coordination site, but is strongly modulated by denticity and geometry (chelate effect, ring size) and by ligand class (phosphates/phosphonates > polycarboxylates > monocarboxylates). Thus, within a ligand family at matched conditions, higher pKa of the relevant O‑acid group correlates with larger Ca2+ logβ, while cross‑family comparisons must account for charge and chelation first.

cross‑family comparisons must account for charge and chelation first
